findings.evidence_quote: Putting aside just $10 per month into the Credit Builder plan could set you on a course towards better credit3 – You could even add 17 points to your score on average in 3 days of starting the plan!4
findings.location: body paragraph about Credit Karma Credit Builder
findings.reasoning: R-04 triggers when an institution's advertisement states a monetary/account-opening bonus (e.g., 'get a $200 bonus when you open an account with X balance'). This page discusses a Credit Builder savings/loan product and cites a statistical average credit-score increase ('17 points on average in 3 days') as a performance outcome of using the product, not a promotional bonus offered for opening an account or meeting a balance/time requirement. There is no mention of any cash bonus, sign-up bonus, or bonus tied to account opening or balance thresholds anywhere on the page. The closest candidate is the '17 points' statistic, but this is a data-backed performance claim about credit score improvement, not a 'bonus' in the deposit-account-bonus sense the rule addresses (which concerns things like $200 for opening a checking account with a $500 minimum balance within 90 days). The rule's own carve-out reinforces that only actual quantified bonus offers trigger the disclosure requirements, and general statements or performance claims don't count. Strongest counter-reading: one could argue the '17 points' claim is itself framed like a bonus incentive to encourage sign-up, blurring the line, but it is denominated in credit-score points, not a monetary/account bonus, and is not conditioned on a minimum balance or a bonus amount for opening an account. Therefore, the rule's trigger (an institution stating a bonus in an advertisement for a deposit/account product) is not met.

Scanned the full article, disclosures, and footer for any advertised bank or deposit-account bonus (e.g., cash bonus, signup bonus, APY-tied bonus). The only financial product marketing present concerns Credit Karma Money Spend and Credit Builder, along with banking-service disclosures, but there is no statement of any bonus or incentive. The word “bonus” appears only in a tool name “Bonus tax calculator,” which is about tax treatment of employment bonuses, not an advertised account bonus. Under the rule’s definition, general statements such as “bonus checking” or unrelated uses of “bonus” do not trigger the disclosure requirements. Therefore, the IF condition of R-04 is not met, the rule is untriggered, and it is not applicable to this page.

Scanned the article and footer/navigation for any statement of a cash/deposit account bonus (e.g., “get $X bonus,” “sign-up bonus,” APY-linked bonus). The page discusses credit scores and mentions Credit Karma Money Credit Builder, but makes no bonus offer. The only occurrence of the word “bonus” is a navigation link to “Bonus tax calculator,” which is not a deposit account promotion. No APY, minimum balance, timing, or payout of any bonus is stated. Therefore, the rule’s trigger (an advertised bonus) is not met.
